title: Huge-project effectiveness test (nestjs/nest, 1683 files): deterministic enforcement is effective + precise at scale; behavioral prevention is situational (capable models often don't commit the violation unprompted)
context: Validate that Hunch is effective on a large REAL codebase (not a toy), both mechanics-at-scale and the Hunch-on/off behavioral test. Cloned nestjs/nest (1683 TS/JS files, 2125 tracked).
decision: Ran three things on the cloned repo with the v0.36.1 built binary. (1) SCALE: hunch init+index in ~1s, building a real graph (5709 symbols, 2812 edges, 6 components); hunch why returns real symbols; hunch check is ~instant. (2) DETERMINISTIC ENFORCEMENT of a REAL architectural invariant ('packages/core must stay HTTP-platform-agnostic — never import express', --forbid-dep express, scope packages/core/**): 5/5 correct — real `import express` BLOCKED; benign edit silent; a COMMENT naming express silent (parsed-import immunity holds at scale); `import type { Express } from express` BLOCKED (type imports caught); the same import in the out-of-scope platform-express package ALLOWED (scope correct). (3) BEHAVIORAL on/off (Sonnet, 6+6 per task, real nest framing): two tasks ('getClientIp' tempting express; 'deepMerge' tempting lodash) both gave 0% violation in BOTH arms — the capable model didn't reach for the dep unprompted (used node:http / hand-rolled native). Injection still measurably shifted behavior (express: ON 6/6 adopted the rule's opaque-type guidance vs OFF 6/6 concrete node:http type), just didn't PREVENT a violation because none occurred.
consequences: Deterministic enforcement (the guard) is the dependable moat and it WORKS at scale: precise (comment-immune, scope-correct, catches type-only imports), fast (~instant over 5709 symbols), on a real architectural invariant. This is the strongest result of the whole arc.

Behavioral prevention is SITUATIONAL: the on/off violation-prevention signal only appears when the violation is one the model is actually tempted to commit. The earlier controlled experiment (lodash INSTALLED + a 'group the orders' use-task) hit 100%->0%; realistic nest 'add a helper' tasks with a capable model produced 0%/0% nulls because the model implemented natively. So injection's value depends on whether the agent would have violated — the deterministic guard is the backstop for when it does.

Methodology note for future behavioral tests: to elicit a violation you must (a) establish the dep as installed/available and (b) phrase the task as 'use X' not 'implement a helper that does X'. Otherwise capable models don't take the bait and the test is a null.
Cross-client delivery remains the untouched next initiative; this test used the Claude-family agent + the deterministic guard (client-agnostic).
